Table 1: Quantitative comparison of Future Forcing and baselines across 30-second and 60-second long video generation on VBenchLong Huang et al. [2024], using MovieGen prompts Polyak et al. [2024]. ↑ denotes higher is better for all metrics. The best results within each group are bold.
Figure 15: Additional visualization results for Self-Forcing in 30-second video generation.

Table 3: Quantitative comparison across models, KV cache compression ratios, and forcing strategies on additional VBench Motion Smoothness and Dynamic Degree. ↑ denotes higher is better.
Table 8: Normalized pairwise Wasserstein distance W1 of pre-RoPE and RoPE-modulated queries.
Figure 16: Additional visualization results for Reward-Forcing in 60-second video generation.
Figure 17: Additional visualization results for Self-Forcing in 60-second video generation.

Table 2: Quantitative comparison of Future Forcing against baselines for 5-second short video generation under reduced KV cache budgets. “CR” denotes the KV cache compression ratio relative to full cache. ↑ indicates higher is better, while ↓ indicates lower is better. Best results are bold. The results show that Future Forcing incurs less information loss under the reduced KV cache budgets.

Figure 2: Query distribution across latent frames for different autoregressive video generation models. (Quantitative results based on mean normalized Wasserstein distance are detailed in Appendix G). While the distributions of RoPE-modulated queries vary noticeably across autoregressive generation stages, the distributions of the corresponding pre-RoPE queries remain considerably more stable.

Figure 3: Overview of Future Forcing, which constructs future query proxies from stable pre-RoPE queries, combines future- and history-aware scores to retain KV pairs, and merges evicted entries into retained ones in future-query profiling space, reducing information loss under a limited cache budget.
